content.full_text:
LAW OFFICES OF
NIELSEN, MERKSAMER,
HODGSON, PARRINELLO & MUELLER
A PARTNERSHIP INCLUDING A PROFESSIONAL CORPORATION

SACRAMENTO                          591 REDWOOD HIGHWAY, #4000                    SAN FRANCISCO
770 L STREET, SUITE 800            MILL VALLEY, CALIFORNIA 94941                 650 CALIFORNIA STREET, SUITE 2650
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814        TELEPHONE (415) 389-6800                      SAN FRANCISCO, CALIFORNIA 94108
TELEPHONE (916) 446-6752                                                          TELEPHONE (415) 989-6800


                                        May 30, 1990




Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Chief, Technical Assistance
and Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814


                    Re:  Confirmation of Telephone Advice

Dear Jeanne:

     This is to confirm the telephone conversation of May 23,
1990 regarding the filing status of our client citizens for Fiscal
and Economic Responsibility (CFER) and its requirement to file late
contribution notices for receipts of $1,000 or more.

                              Facts

     We advised you that CFER had been organized in December,
1989 to oppose two Nuclear Free ordinances which were then being
circulated for the Alameda County and San Francisco County June
1990 ballots.  The committee at the time was headquartered in San
Francisco.  Petitioners circulating the Alameda County measure did
submit sufficient signatures and did qualify for the June 5, 1990
ballot.  Petition circulators for the San Francisco measure ceased
circulation for the June ballot and re-submitted petitions and are
currently circulating for qualification for the November 1990 San
Francisco ballot.  Our committee, on its Statement of Organization,
stated that it was primarily formed to oppose both of these
measures.  In addition to opposing these two measures, our committee
has made two monetary contributions to the campaign sign committee
opposing a Nuclear Free ordinance amendment in Marin County.  The
Marin contributions have accounted for approximately 7% of total
expenditures made by our committee to date.  The committee has
spent approximately 10% of its resources in strategizing to defeat
the possible San Francisco measure, and the remaining 83% of the
committee's resources have been spent to oppose the Alameda County
Measure.

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Page 2


                              Conclusions


     1.   CFER  is  not  considered  a  "primarily  formed
          committee" as that term is defined in Government
          Code Section §82047.5:

          "Primarily formed committee" means a committee pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 82013
          which is formed or exists primarily to support or oppose a single candidate or a single
          measure, or a group of specific candidates being voted upon in the same city or county
          election. (Emphasis added)


     2.   CFER  is  considered  a  state  general  purpose
          committee, as defined in Government Code Section
          §82027.5 (b):

          A "State General Purpose Committee" is a committee to support or oppose candidates or measures
          voted on in a state election or in more than one county". (Emphasis added)


     3.   Since CFER is not a "primarily formed committee"
          and is a "state general purpose committee",  it
          would legally mean that it is not required to file
          notices of late contributions disclosing receipts
          of $1,000 or more during the period May 20 through
          June 4, 1990.   Because only contributions to
          primarily  formed  committees  can  be  "late
          contributions", you confirmed that nominally no
          contributor to CFER would be required to file a late
          contribution notice in connection with the June 5,
          1990 Alameda County election. These two conclusions
          result because "late contribution" is defined in
          Government Code Section §82036 to be:

          ...(A)ny contribution including a loan which totals in the aggregate one thousand dollars
          ($1,000) or more that is made to or received by a candidate, a controlled committee, or a
          committee formed or existing primarily to support or oppose a candidate or measure before the
          date of the election at which the candidate or measure is to be voted on but after the closing
          date of the last campaign statement required to be filed before the election. (Emphasis added)

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Page 3


                         You explained that this result was noticed by the
                    FPPC for the first time in 1988, when a number of
                    ballot measure committees were formed to support or
                    oppose a number of state ballot measures especially
                    ones formed to influence the voters on the four
                    insurance initiatives (Proposition 100, 103, 104 &
                    106).  Such committees were not "primarily formed
                    committees" and therefore were not required to file
                    late contribution notices pursuant to Government
                    Section §82036.  This pattern of multiple state
                    measures being supported by a single committee (or
                    in our case, two measures in more than one county)
                    has resulted in the FPPC's request to Assemblyman
                    Ted Lempert to introduce a legislative amendment to
                    the Political Reform Act which is currently being
                    considered by the Legislature.  This bill, if
                    enacted, would require late contribution notices for
                    committees which are formed to support or oppose one
                    or more measures on the same ballot.

            4.  We then discussed the fairly unique status of CFER
                where the two measures will be on two separate
                ballots.  We advised you that CFER, based upon its
                activity considered itself to be a " state general
                purpose committee".  But because the "spirit" of the
                law is to provide local disclosure in connection with
                the June election, we previously told you that we
                had decided to also file our disclosure reports in
                Alameda County.  To do this we moved the Committee's
                headquarters to Oakland, and therefore, we are now
                filing two copies of our report with the Registrar
                of Voters in Alameda County as our country of
                domicile.  This is in addition to filing reports
                with the Secretary of State and the Registrars in
                San Francisco and Los Angeles Counties.

            5.  We next discussed whether because of our unique
                circumstances we should file late contribution
                reports.  Although not required by the Act as
                presently enacted, our client agreed voluntarily to
                provide local pre-election disclosure and file late
                contribution notices with our regular filing
                officials, including Alameda County, for all
                contributions received of $1,000 or more during the
                "late" period before the June 1990 election.  CFER
                has also advised contributors that they should
                consider contributions to our committee of $1,000
                or more to be "late" contributions and to file
                appropriately.

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Page 4


     If this letter accurately reflects our conversation,
please confirm this in writing at your earliest convenience. If
you have any questions or need any clarification, please do not
hesitate to contact me.

                                        Kind regards,

                                        [signature]
                                        Kirk Alan Pessner
                                        Paralegal/Administrator
                                        Political Reports Unit

KAP/en

sections.conclusion:
1. CFER is not considered a "primarily formed committee" as that term is defined in Government Code Section §82047.5:

"Primarily formed committee" means a committee pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 82013 which is formed or exists primarily to support or oppose a single candidate or a single measure, or a group of specific candidates being voted upon in the same city or county election. (Emphasis added)

2. CFER is considered a state general purpose committee, as defined in Government Code Section §82027.5 (b):

A "State General Purpose Committee" is a committee to support or oppose candidates or measures voted on in a state election or in more than one county. (Emphasis added)

3. Since CFER is not a "primarily formed committee" and is a "state general purpose committee", it would legally mean that it is not required to file notices of late contributions disclosing receipts of $1,000 or more during the period May 20 through June 4, 1990. Because only contributions to primarily formed committees can be "late contributions", you confirmed that normally no contributor to CFER would be required to file a late contribution notice in connection with the June 5, 1990 Alameda County election. These two conclusions result because "late contribution" is defined in Government Code Section §82036 to be:

...(A)ny contribution including a loan which totals in the aggregate one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more that is made to or received by a candidate, a controlled committee, or a committee formed or existing primarily to support or oppose a candidate or measure before the date of the election at which the candidate or measure is to be voted on but after the closing date of the last campaign statement required to be filed before the election. (Emphasis added)

You explained that this result was noticed by the FPPC for the first time in 1988, when a number of ballot measure committees were formed to support or oppose a number of state ballot measures especially ones formed to influence the voters on the four insurance initiatives (Proposition 100, 103, 104 & 106). Such committees were not "primarily formed committees" and therefore were not required to file late contribution notices pursuant to Government Section §82036. This pattern of multiple state measures being supported by a single committee (or in our case, two measures in more than one county) has resulted in the FPPC's request to Assemblyman Ted Lempert to introduce a legislative amendment to the Political Reform Act which is currently being considered by the Legislature. This bill, if enacted, would require late contribution notices for committees which are formed to support or oppose one or more measures on the same ballot.

4. We then discussed the fairly unique status of CFER where the two measures will be on two separate ballots. We advised you that CFER, based upon its activity considered itself to be a "state general purpose committee". But because the "spirit" of the law is to provide local disclosure in connection with the June election, we previously told you that we had decided to also file our disclosure reports in Alameda County. To do this we moved the Committee's headquarters to Oakland, and therefore, we are now filing two copies of our report with the Registrar of Voters in Alameda County as our county of domicile. This is in addition to filing reports with the Secretary of State and the Registrars in San Francisco and Los Angeles Counties.

5. We next discussed whether because of our unique circumstances we should file late contribution reports. Although not required by the Act as presently enacted, our client agreed voluntarily to provide local pre-election disclosure and file late contribution notices with our regular filing officials, including Alameda County, for all contributions received of $1,000 or more during the "late" period before the June 1990 election. Also CFER has advised contributors that they should consider contributions to our committee of $1,000 or more to be "late" contributions and to file appropriately.

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard

If this letter accurately reflects our conversation, please confirm this in writing at your earliest convenience. If you have any questions or need any clarification, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Kind regards,

Kirk Alan Pessner
Paralegal/Administrator
Political Reports Unit

The committee at the time was headquartered in San Francisco. Petitioners circulating the Alameda County measure did submit sufficient signatures and did qualify for the June 5, 1990 ballot. Petition circulators for the San Francisco measure ceased circulation for the June ballot and re-submitted petitions and are currently circulating for qualification for the November 1990 San Francisco ballot. Our committee, on its Statement of Organization, stated that it was primarily formed to oppose both of these measures. In addition to opposing these two measures, our committee has made two monetary contributions to the campaign committee opposing a Nuclear Free ordinance amendment in Marin County. The Marin contributions have accounted for approximately 7% of total expenditures made by our committee to date. The committee has spent approximately 10% of its resources in strategizing to defeat the possible San Francisco measure, and the remaining 83% of the committee's resources have been spent to oppose the Alameda County Measure.

"Primarily formed committee" means a committee pursuant to subdivision (a) of Section 82013 which is formed or exists primarily to support or oppose a single candidate or a single measure, or a group of specific candidates being voted upon in the same city or county election. (Emphasis added)

Because only contributions to primarily formed committees can be "late contributions", you confirmed that normally no contributor to CFER would be required to file a late contribution notice in connection with the June 5, 1990 Alameda County election. These two conclusions result because "late contribution" is defined in Government Code Section §82036 to be:

You explained that this result was noticed by the FPPC for the first time in 1988, when a number of ballot measure committees were formed to support or oppose a number of state ballot measures especially ones formed to influence the voters on the four insurance initiatives (Proposition 100, 103, 104 & 106). Such committees were not "primarily formed committees" and therefore were not required to file late contribution notices pursuant to Government Section §82036. This bill, if enacted, would require late contribution notices for committees which are formed to support or oppose one or more measures on the same ballot.

Also CFER has advised contributors that they should consider contributions to our committee of $1,000 or more to be "late" contributions and to file appropriately.

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard

If this letter accurately reflects our conversation, please confirm this in writing at your earliest convenience. If you have any questions or need any clarification, please do not hesitate to contact me.

Kind regards,

Kirk Alan Pessner
Paralegal/Administrator
Political Reports Unit
